author: nflaig
content:
regarding https://github.com/ethereum/beacon-APIs/pull/580, it currently carries over the `builder_boost_factor` from produce block v3. I think it is still useful in gloas same as today and would work almost the same ie. you apply a multiplier to the local payload when comparing it against the highest bid from p2p and optionally from builder api.

so I think the default should be to keep `builder_boost_factor` in produce block v4 and carry it over from v3

I would like to hear arguments for removing it in gloas/v4

cc <@&1417820113981145228> <@1386598056320831509>

author: nflaig
content: builder_boost_factor on produce block v4

author: nflaig
content: maybe could use the opportunity to rename it to `local_boost_factor` as I find the naming confusing but for backward compatiblity sake maybe not

author: bharath.vedartham
content:
the flow would look like:
1. We fetch the best bid from the builder-api after applying the min-bid and the `max_trusted_bid`
2. We fetch the best bid from the p2p.
3. We evaluate the best bid from the builder-api and the p2p
4. Apply the `builder_boost_factor` with the local bid and the above bid selected across the builder-api and p2p

author: bharath.vedartham
content: yeah the `local_boost_factor` makes sense to me too. Basically some way to prioritise the local block value

author: tersec
content: last time this was tried, there was a well-intentioned campaign to get CLs to default to a 10% (or similar, some modest) local boost. And Nimbus did, actually. It doesn't matter because the builders are typically much better than that

author: bharath.vedartham
content: yeah i think this is a good lever to only accept a builder block if it really outweighs the local block by a significant amount. Most builder blocks are around ~0.01Eth, there could be cases where the local block is picked which is better for decentralization

author: bharath.vedartham
content: not too opininanated about the defaults, but definitely a good flag to have for the protocol IMO

author: tersec
content: Unfortunately the result was useless complexity

author: nflaig
content: this was a while ago but got some data point here https://github.com/ChainSafe/lodestar/issues/6602#issuecomment-2025108408, so the 10% made barely any difference

author: tersec
content:
I still regard Nimbus going along with that as a (small) wart. And the next time someone came along with another feel-good tweak with a similar idea, I did not add it to Nimbus, because it had the same flaw, that it was proposed in a vacuum, with no empirical evidence it would work, no measurements. And this is substantially what motivated having this feature to begin with.

The other motivations:

- always select engine. Useless in pre-Gloas (just don't configure MEV). This one is more interesting in Gloas actually because one can't avoid the builders by just doing nothing.

- always select builder. This one I'm unsure of, because the same entities which want always-builder at least often seem to want some _specific_ set of builders

author: tersec
content: exactly yeah, it's useful to have the data indeed

author: nflaig
content: we also have the 10% boost for local by default, and I found some local blocks we did due to that on our lido validators, it got a bit more effective since mev went down in general

author: tersec
content: And the problem with the higher boosts is the more opinionated they get the more individual validator sacrifice they require. The additional risk a proposers take on (which is an argument for this in general) starts being outweighed by, no, there's not a 20+% chance the builder will renege

author: tersec
content: or even a 5% chance

author: tersec
content: And in ePBS the risk is mostly gone anyway, because the CL can get its block on chain regardless

author: nflaig
content: I think the flag is mostly useful as a boolean tbh, but for that, I think the vc should decide whether it wants a local or builder payload and the only way to signal that is via the api

author: tersec
content: that's my general inclination, yes. There does need to be some API. But I'm not at all sure the builder/local boost is the right lever

author: tersec
content: But regarding the always-builder, that only makes sense when an external entity demands/requires it, e.g., as part of a staking pool, or are there other use cases?

author: nflaig
content: well today you would still propose a local block if the builders fail to deliver a header

author: nflaig
content: I am not too sure about actual use case for this

author: tersec
content: Yes, that's what I'd want to understand

author: tersec
content: I have seen cases of it, but in all the cases I've personally seen it comes with additional requirements on _which_ builders

author: tersec
content: which this API does not capture anyway

author: tersec
content: i.e. it's a staking pool control mechanism and if they want control they want actual control

author: tersec
content: or DVT

author: nflaig
content: we have these settings on the vc in lodestar https://chainsafe.github.io/lodestar/run/validator-management/vc-configuration#configure-your-builder-selection-andor-builder-boost-factor

author: nflaig
content: so I guess the question is, why should all these settings go away with gloas

author: tersec
content: Fulu-the-standard doesn't have all of those settings either

author: nflaig
content: you can express all besides `builderonly` (which is stupid) with `builder_boost_factor`?

author: nflaig
content: so I guess the only really useful one is the ability for the vc to tell the bn to prefer the local payload if viable, no matter what the bid values are

author: tersec
content: That one yeah, with Gloas that's useful in a way that it's not in Fulu

author: nflaig
content: so I guess my question mostly is what do we think about keeping `builder_boost_factor` as is, to me it seems to still have the same purpose and is already implemented in clients

author: bharath.vedartham
content: a part of me also feels like the `max_trusted_bid` might go down this same path. What is the right value to set to it. It makes sense to cap the execution payment in the builder-api as builders we don't trust might not reveal the payload. Would people just end up configuring it as `MAX_UINT64` if they want trusted payments otherwise `0` if they want only trustless.
